primary_diagnosis.name: Indolent systemic mastocytosis with an acute mast cell mediator-release flare, without anaphylaxis

differential.diagnosis: mast cell mediator-release flare in known mastocytosis
differential.evidence_basis: Known clonal mast cell disorder with rapid friction-triggered urticarial eruption, flushing and bone pain — a classic degranulation flare.
Prior recurrent anaphylaxis and active systemic degranulation mean anaphylaxis MUST be actively screened for before it can be set aside.
Widespread urticaria could be simple acute allergic urticaria, but does not explain the bone pain or the friction-provoked wheal on pigmented lesions.

differential.diagnosis: indolent mast cell mediator-release flare (no anaphylaxis)
differential.evidence_basis: Negative airway/circulatory/GI screen with self-reported comfortable breathing and no presyncope — cutaneous + skeletal mediator flare with preserved haemodynamics.
ASCIA criteria NOT met: no airway compromise, no respiratory distress, no hypotension/collapse, no persistent GI symptoms. Remains the standing safety-net risk but is not the current event.
Friction-provoked wheal on chronic pigmented lesions and the known underlying disorder are not features of a viral rash.

differential.diagnosis: indolent systemic mastocytosis — acute mediator-release flare, no anaphylaxis, haemodynamically stable
differential.evidence_basis: Stable vitals, patent airway, negative red-flag screen; cutaneous and skeletal mediator symptoms in an established clonal mast cell disorder. Bone marrow mast cell infiltrate explains diffuse femoral ache; bone metastases excluded by the diffuse, degranulation-timed, non-focal pattern.

red_flags.description: Upper-airway compromise — tongue/throat swelling, voice change or inspiratory stridor (feature of anaphylaxis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would reclassify the event as anaphylaxis with an immediate airway threat (T5). Its ABSENCE on direct screening is why this is NOT a 000 dispatch.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If airway involvement were present and missed, fatal airway obstruction could follow within minutes.

red_flags.description: Circulatory compromise — faintness/dizziness on standing, collapse, or hypotension (distributive/anaphylactic shock)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would indicate anaphylactic shock (T5). Absent here — patient reports no presyncope and clinician-measured BP is normal.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected shock progresses to cardiovascular collapse.

red_flags.description: Persistent GI involvement — repeated vomiting, cramping abdominal pain (another anaphylaxis limb)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absent — supports a mediator flare rather than multi-system anaphylaxis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Multi-system involvement missed could under-call an anaphylactic reaction.

red_flags.description: Standing lifelong risk of sudden, unpredictable anaphylaxis inherent to systemic mastocytosis, patient lives alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: This baseline risk is why prominent, mandatory anaphylaxis safety-netting (immediate autoinjector + 000) is essential even though the current event is a stable flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net leaves an isolated high-risk patient without a rescue plan if the flare tips into anaphylaxis.

pathophysiology_summary: In systemic mastocytosis there is a clonal expansion of mast cells (commonly KIT D816V-driven) infiltrating skin and bone marrow. Mediator release — histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes — is provoked here by minor physical friction, producing urticaria pigmentosa lesions that wheal on stroking (Darier's sign), systemic flushing and mild tachycardia, plus deep bone pain from marrow mast cell burden. Crucially, this is a MEDIATOR flare, not IgE anaphylaxis: haemodynamics are preserved, the airway is patent and there is no circulatory collapse. The same disease, however, carries a lifelong risk of true anaphylaxis, so the correct posture is symptomatic mediator control (H1 + H2 blockade, trigger/NSAID avoidance, cold compress) with hard anaphylaxis safety-netting, not emergency dispatch for the stable flare.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the prior-anaphylaxis history and the patient's terror into declaring anaphylaxis and dispatching 000, despite a negative airway/circulatory/GI screen and stable haemodynamics — the central over-triage trap of this case.
Reaching for an NSAID to treat the femoral bone ache — NSAIDs are a recognised mast cell degranulation trigger and can worsen or precipitate a reaction. A dangerous error of commission.

Correctly recognising the stable flare but failing to give explicit, mandatory anaphylaxis safety-netting to an isolated patient with lifelong unpredictable anaphylaxis risk — the opposite under-triage error.
Interpreting diffuse bone pain as metastatic disease and over-investigating, rather than recognising marrow mast cell infiltrate tied to the degranulation timeline.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Precise blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and SpO2 (no home device offered)
Darier's sign elicited on examination (stroking lesions to provoke wheal)
Auscultation of the chest / true air entry
Serum mast cell tryptase and full blood count
Bone marrow biopsy with KIT D816V testing (definitive systemic-disease confirmation)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a stable mast cell mediator-release flare (NOT current anaphylaxis) on the negative red-flag screen; give symptomatic self-care advice, optimise oral H1 + H2 antihistamines, advise strict NSAID/trigger avoidance, ensure an in-date adrenaline autoinjector is on hand, and arrange prompt GP + immunology review. Escalate to 000 ONLY if airway, circulatory or GI red-flag symptoms develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Bidirectional. The dominant risk is OVER-action — treating the stable flare as anaphylaxis and dispatching an ambulance, or reflexively over-escalating because telehealth 'cannot examine'. The opposite risk is UNDER-action — dismissing the isolated, anaphylaxis-prone patient without hard safety-netting. The correct answer holds both: stable-flare management PLUS mandatory anaphylaxis escalation triggers.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Anaphylaxis requires rapid-onset airway/breathing and/or circulation involvement; intramuscular adrenaline is first-line. Absence of these limbs, with isolated skin/mediator symptoms and stable haemodynamics, does not meet anaphylaxis criteria.

Mediator-symptom control uses H1 and H2 antihistamines and trigger avoidance; NSAIDs and other degranulators are avoided unless individually tolerated; patients carry adrenaline autoinjectors for anaphylaxis risk.
